title: 8 Tiqimt — The Birthday of the Virgin Mary & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "8 Tiqimt — The Birthday of the Virgin Mary & The Assembly

narrative": "Tiqimt

The Birthday of the Blessed Virgin Mary (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Moses the Black (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Thais the Penitent

The eighth day of Tiqimt returns the community to the monthly commemoration of the Virgin Mary's Birthday, and in Tiqimt's interior light the feast of Mary's birth carries the quality of the hidden and the ordinary. She was born in a household in Nazareth or Jerusalem — the tradition differs on the location — to two aging parents whose years of prayer had been answered in a child they had never expected. There were no angels in the room at her birth, no star over the house, no Magi traveling from the East. It was a birth like other births, extraordinary only in the perspective of retrospect — which is precisely Tiqimt's mode of vision. The month of interiority sees what the moment of celebration cannot see: the significance of the ordinary, the holiness hidden in the unremarkable, the divine purposes at work in the domestic scene that the world will not recognize as sacred until long after the fact.

The monthly commemoration of St. Moses the Black arrives in Tiqimt with a quality of settled wisdom. The former bandit, the violent man who was entirely transformed by grace, who became one of the most sought-after spiritual guides in the Egyptian desert — in the second month's quieter light, Moses speaks most clearly about the interior life of repentance as a sustained practice rather than a single dramatic event. He is known for saying that a man must die before his death — must undergo the interior dying that makes the external death, when it comes, unremarkable. This practice of interior dying is Tiqimt's characteristic discipline: the deliberate, daily laying down of the ego's claims, the repetition of small deaths that constitute the contemplative life.

St. Thais the Penitent is one of the most beloved of all the desert's penitent women — a celebrated courtesan of Alexandria or Antioch who was sought out by the Abba Paphnutius, who came to her disguised as a customer and confronted her with the reality of God's knowledge of her life. She was so struck by his words that she burned her wealth and presented herself to a monastery, where she was walled into a cell with only a small opening for food, without the ordinary liturgical prayers, living in the cell's darkness and repetition of a single prayer: 'You who made me, have mercy on me.' She was released from the cell after three years, was told in a vision by Paphnutius that her repentance had been received, and died peacefully two weeks later. Thais is the patron of those whose conversion began with a confrontation they did not seek, and whose penitential practice was brutal in its simplicity and complete in its efficacy.

Mary's birthday, Moses, and Thais together on the eighth day of Tiqimt present the community with three modes of the hidden holy. Mary is holy from birth, unrecognized until the retrospect of history reveals what was always present. Moses is holy through transformation, the bandit's violence converted into pastoral tenderness by the same grace that is being offered monthly in the calendar's commemoration. Thais is holy through the single-prayer simplicity of a cell — the reduction of the entire interior life to one cry repeated without ceasing, which turns out to be sufficient. The Covenant of Mercy that defines this month is the covenant that holds all three: the one born holy, the one transformed into holiness, and the one who discovered holiness in the smallest possible space.
